title: practical english usage - Part 41
content:
(the speaker's knowledge) Don't lend him  your car. He's a terrible driver - he'll crash it.

- Look out - we're going to crash! (There is outside evidence.) something; we  are asking him or her to believe something.) Compare: believe, or have calculated. (When we  use will, we  are not showing the listener when we  are talking more about what is inside our heads: what we  know, or We   prefer will for predictions when there is not such obvious outside evidence - rt to tztrw/ ji fait' not Look - that kid's going to fall off his bike, (     Look! That-kidmfaU off not See those clouds? It's going to rain, (     See-tkose elouds?4t-wiU rain.) say - for example black clouds in the sky, a person who  is obviously about to fall.

In predictions, we  use going to when we  have outside evidence for what we 2 predictions:       thinking      and   guessing       about     the   future / hope it will be better than the last one. (not talking about the present) - We're going to get a new car. (The decision already exists.) I wonder if she'll recognise me. (not talking about the present) - I'm seeing Jessica on Tuesday. (The arrangement exists now.) cases we  use will. Compare: talking about future events that have some present reality (►  35-36). In other We   prefer present forms (present progressive or going to . ..) when we  are 1  present      reality a good reason for using present forms.

Will is the basic structure for talking about the future. We   use will if there is not a dvanced      points    _______________________ 39 will,    going       to   and   present      progressive: grammar •  39   will, going to and present progressive: advanced points You will start work at six o'clock sharp.

The regiment will attack at dawn.

in military-style orders.

to do something, the speaker just says firmly that it will happen. This is common Predictions can be used as a way of giving orders - instead of telling somebody 5  predictions        as  orders:    The    regiment          will    attack       at   dawn.

14.30, and will then attend a performance of traditional dances.

The Princess will arrive at the airport at 14.00. She will meet the President at We're meeting Sandra at 6.00.

impersonal, fixed arrangements - for example official itineraries. Compare: Will is often used, rather than present forms, in giving information about at   14.30.

4 official     arrangements:         The     Princess         will    meet      the    President which  one is chosen.

In cases like these, the different forms are all correct, and it is unimportant I'm really going to stop smoking! I really will stop smoking!

or determination.

Both going to ... (►   35) and stressed will (►  79.1) can express a strong intention Jack's going to explain everything to you.

- Jack will explain everything to you.

Next year is going to be different.

- Next year will be different.

You're not going to believe this.

- You won't believe this.

If your mother comes, you're going to have to help with the cooking.

- If your mother comes, you'll have to help with the cooking.

All the family are going to be there.

- All the family will be there.

What are you going to do next year? (emphasis on intentions) What are you doing next year? (emphasis on fixed arrangements) clear plans have been made) - What will you do next year? (open question about the future; perhaps no depend on which  aspect we  wish to emphasise.

arrangement are a part of the meaning, but not very important. The  choice can both possible in the same situation, if ’ present* ideas like intention or fixed always very clear-cut.  Will and present forms (especially going to ...) are often The  differences between the structures used to talk about the future are not 3 will    and   present      forms:    both    possible Section 4 Talking     about     the   Future grammar •  40  future perfect ►     39.7.

For will have... used to express certainty about the past (e.g. It's no use phoning - he'll have left by now), I'll have been teaching for twenty years this summer.
